Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Philip Pettit

b. 1945

Intentional agency, self-regulation, and the norm-governed mind.

Philip Pettit does not give us a single treatise called “A Philosophy of Mind.” What he gives us is something more scattered and, in some ways, more interesting: a body of work on intentionality, functionalism, folk psychology, mental causation, language, self-regulation, group agency, consciousness, freedom, personhood, respect, and republican political theory. His 2025 book When Minds Converse: A Social Genealogy of the Human Soul makes explicit what had been implicit for decades: Pettit’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophical anthropology of the “human soul,” provided that “soul” is not read as an immaterial substance but as the socially generated, language-mediated form of minded life distinctive of persons. Pettit’s central claim, reconstructed across the corpus, is that human mindedness is not simply an inner Cartesian theater, nor merely a biological capacity, nor a private stream of qualia. It is a natural, functional, socially disciplined, linguistically transformed, norm-governed, self-regulating, and often collective phenomenon.

The most compact formulation is this: for Pettit, a mind is a natural intentional system capable of representation and goal-directed action; a human mind is such a system transformed by language, judgment, reason-giving, commitment, self-regulation, accountability, and personhood; consciousness, in its most philosophically tractable form, is “coawareness,” the state in which intentional contents are present to a subject as reasons; and the “soul” is the socially sustained complex of capacities by which speaking beings become judging, reasoning, responsible, self-identifying persons. This is not theology in the traditional sense, but it is not a merely deflationary metaphor either. Pettit is trying to give a naturalistic account of what older religious and metaphysical vocabularies often tried to mark: the special status of human persons.

1. The architecture of Pettit’s view

Pettit’s philosophy of mind rests on several linked theses.

First, he is a naturalist. Minds are not made out of special mental stuff. In “The Self-Regulating Mind,” written with Victoria McGeer, Pettit says that an intentional system is one whose representations and actions reliably conform to the environment and to goals; this account “does not mean that the system is possessed of mental stuff, a mental substance, or any distinctively mental causal powers.” The point is not to deny mind, but to deny a spooky ontology of mind. Human beings, nonhuman animals, and perhaps artificial systems may count as minded in some sense, but human minds are distinctive because they can become self-regulating systems that represent norms or constraints as constraints and regulate themselves accordingly.

Second, he is not an eliminativist. Pettit and Frank Jackson defend the existence and explanatory legitimacy of beliefs and desires against the Churchlands’ eliminativist suspicion that folk psychology might go the way of phlogiston or ether. Their paper “In Defence of Folk Psychology” explicitly frames the issue in those terms and argues that the eliminativist analogy fails: beliefs and desires are not dispensable pseudo-entities but real, functional, explanatory states.

Third, he is a functionalist, but not a narrow internalist. In “Functionalism and Broad Content,” Jackson and Pettit emphasize that many intentional states have contents that are “broad” or context-bound: what someone believes, desires, or hopes can depend not only on what is inside the agent but also on worldly relations and environmental embedding. This already pushes Pettit away from any philosophy of mind that tries to locate all mental reality inside the skull. Mind is realized in organisms, but intentional content is world-involving.

Fourth, he is a non-reductive explanatory pluralist. In “Program Explanation,” Jackson and Pettit argue that a higher-level property can be causally relevant even when it is not the directly efficacious microphysical property that produces an effect. A higher-level property may “program” for the presence of lower-level causal realizers. This gives mental, social, and normative explanations a genuine explanatory role without requiring them to compete with physics at the same level. The point is crucial: Pettit can be a physicalist or naturalist without making mental explanation epiphenomenal. Beliefs, desires, commitments, institutional roles, and collective intentions may not be extra forces in nature, but they can still structure, program, and explain what happens.

Fifth, he is a social holist but an individualist in ontology and politics. The Common Mind asks what makes human beings intentional and thinking subjects and how this connects with their social nature. Its stated aim is to show that human thought requires communal resources without compromising individual autonomy, a position Pettit calls “holistic individualism.” Non-English reception has been helpful here. A French discussion of Pettit characterizes his view as “weak holism”: society is not a super-agent that absorbs individuals, but it is a condition without which individuals would not develop language, culture, or rational thought. The same French analysis stresses that Pettit’s holism refuses radical holism and refuses the absorption of individual autonomy into society.

Sixth, in his later work, especially When Minds Converse, Pettit radicalizes this social thesis into a genealogy of the human soul. The book’s central hypothesis is that the capacities that distinguish us—judgment, reasoning, perception as appearance-sensitive, values, responsibility, personhood, and self-identity—are not simply pre-social mental endowments that then get expressed in speech. Rather, they emerge as skills mastered in practices of converse: in speaking, challenging, answering, committing, revising, holding responsible, and identifying oneself and others as persons. Pettit’s formulation is deliberately reversal-like: “we do not speak because our human minds are special; our minds are special because we can speak.”

That is the overarching picture: natural mind becomes human soul through language-mediated social discipline.

2. Minimal mind: intentional systems without mental substance

Pettit begins from an intentional-system conception of mind. A minded system represents its environment and acts in pursuit of goals in ways that are sensitive to those representations. This is a broadly functionalist conception. It does not require introspective consciousness, selfhood, language, or personhood. Animals can be minded. Some artificial systems might be minded in a thin sense. A system counts as intentional insofar as it displays the relevant pattern of representation-guided action.

The importance of this starting point is that Pettit’s mature “soul” theory is not dualist. He does not start with a metaphysical gap between matter and mind and then try to bridge it. He starts with systems embedded in nature, some of which can be interpreted as representing and acting. In “The Self-Regulating Mind,” McGeer and Pettit explicitly say that human beings, nonhuman animals, and artificial systems can count as minded in some sense, while insisting that human mindedness has a distinctive self-regulating form.

The first layer of Pettit’s philosophy of mind can therefore be called natural intentionalism. A mind is not a ghostly interior substance but an organized pattern of representation, goal-orientation, and action. This makes Pettit compatible with much contemporary cognitive science. But he differs from reductive computationalism because he insists on the normative, social, and linguistic transformation of mind at the human level.

Theological significance appears already at this level. If “soul” means a nonphysical entity injected into matter, Pettit’s system offers little support for it. But if “soul” names the form of minded life by which a being becomes rational, accountable, and personal, Pettit is deeply concerned with the soul. He naturalizes the soul; he does not trivialize it.

3. Folk psychology: beliefs and desires are real

Pettit’s defense of folk psychology is foundational. He does not treat everyday psychological language as a primitive, incorrigible ontology. But neither does he think neuroscience will simply eliminate beliefs, desires, judgments, intentions, and commitments. His defense is functionalist and explanatory: the entities posited by folk psychology earn their keep because they occupy stable roles in the explanation and regulation of behavior.

“In Defence of Folk Psychology” is directed against the idea that beliefs and desires might be discarded as failed theoretical posits. Jackson and Pettit discuss the Churchlands’ analogy between folk psychology and obsolete scientific entities such as phlogiston or ether, but reject the analogy. The implicit lesson is that the mind is not whatever future neuroscience alone says it is. Neuroscience matters, but the mental vocabulary of belief, desire, judgment, intention, commitment, and responsibility tracks real patterns at another level of organization.

This is where “program explanation” becomes indispensable. Mental properties can explain without being microphysical causes. A belief may not be a little internal object pushing behavior around. Instead, the presence of a belief can program for a range of lower-level realizations and behavioral dispositions. Jackson and Pettit argue that higher-level properties can be causally relevant by ensuring or programming the lower-level conditions that produce an effect; such explanations can remain significant even when a lower-level process explanation is available, because they give modal information about how events would have unfolded under variations.

The payoff is a non-reductive naturalism. Pettit does not say, “Beliefs are immaterial causes.” Nor does he say, “Beliefs are nothing but neural firings, so folk psychology is false.” He says, in effect: beliefs and desires are real functional states whose explanatory force is often programmatic. They organize patterns, possibilities, and counterfactual dependencies. That makes them indispensable for understanding minded agency.

4. Content is broad: mind is world-involving

Pettit’s intentionalism is not skull-bound. In “Functionalism and Broad Content,” Jackson and Pettit argue that the contents of intentional states are often broad or context-dependent. A person’s belief or desire may depend for its identity on external objects, linguistic practices, social histories, or environmental relations.

This matters because Pettit’s later social philosophy of mind is not a sudden break from his early functionalism. It is an expansion of it. If mental content is partly constituted by relations to the world, then it is already plausible that human mental content is partly constituted by relations to language and community. Pettit’s philosophy of mind is externalist not only about reference but about rational subjectivity. The mind’s contents are not sealed within private consciousness.

A useful distinction is this: Pettit’s minimal intentional systems may represent their environments in ways that support action, but human beings operate with contents that are publicly articulable, challengeable, and shareable. Our beliefs are not merely internal states that happen to be expressible. They are often states whose form and content are stabilized through public language.

This is why his view cannot be adequately described as individualist functionalism. It is functionalism socialized and linguisticized.

5. From The Common Mind to “holistic individualism”

The Common Mind is one of the central texts for reconstructing Pettit’s philosophy of mind. Its abstract frames the problem directly: what makes human beings intentional and thinking subjects, and how is this connected to their social nature? Pettit’s answer is that human thought requires communal resources without sacrificing individual autonomy. He calls this “holistic individualism.”

This phrase is easy to misunderstand. “Holistic” does not mean that society is a mystical superorganism. “Individualism” does not mean that individuals are self-sufficient atoms. Pettit’s position is that individual intentional agents are real and autonomous, but the capacities that make them the kind of agents they are depend on social resources: language, norms, practices of interpretation, shared standards of reason, and institutions of accountability.

The French reception of Pettit is especially useful here because it resists both caricatures. The OpenEdition discussion presents Pettit’s position as a weak holism in which society is a condition for the development of language, culture, and rational thought, while individuals remain more real than society and are not absorbed by a radical holist whole. Portuguese reception has also treated Pettit’s work under the heading of “epistemologia holista-individualista,” linking his holist-individualist epistemology to his liberal republicanism.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it gives Pettit a middle path between Cartesian privacy and collectivist absorption. The human mind is socially formed but individually owned. A person’s thought depends on communal practices, but the person is not merely an outlet of the community. This balance will later become central to his account of freedom as non-domination: one can be socially constituted without being socially dominated.

6. Language as the maker of the distinctively human mind

Pettit’s work on Hobbes, especially Made with Words: Hobbes on Language, Mind, and Politics, is not a historical side project. It is a key stage in his philosophy of mind. Pettit reads Hobbes as a thinker for whom human mental, social, and political life is built from matter plus language. A Notre Dame review summarizes Pettit’s interpretation: the book places language at the center of Hobbes’s science of politics, and on Pettit’s reading distinctly human mind emerges from natural mind through language. Language extends cognition from particular to general thought and motivation from bodily to more distinctly mental activity.

This Hobbesian materialism is decisive. Pettit’s human mind is not a Cartesian res cogitans. It is a natural mind transformed by words. The same review notes that, on Pettit’s account of Hobbes, reasoning and personhood depend on language; reason is not a distinct metaphysical faculty, and persons are not inner centers of thought but roles in which commitments are given and taken on behalf of oneself or others.

Spanish-language reception of Made with Words notices this anti-Cartesian dimension: Pettit highlights language as a human skill or creation rather than as something natural or intrinsic in a simple pre-social sense. That emphasis fits perfectly with the later When Minds Converse. The human soul is “made with words” because language does not simply report pre-existing rationality. It creates the space in which rationality, commitment, personation, and accountability can stabilize.

This is one of the most important claims in the whole reconstruction: Pettit reverses the normal order. We are not first fully rational souls who then invent language as a tool. Rather, linguistic converse is one of the enabling conditions under which a natural animal becomes a rational, responsible, self-identifying person.

7. When Minds Converse: a social genealogy of the human soul

When Minds Converse makes Pettit’s philosophy of mind most explicit. The book asks whether the capacities that distinguish human beings derive from human nature alone or from socially mastered skills. Pettit defends a society-first view of the human mind and soul: the relevant capacities emerge first in social practices and only secondarily become internal mental capacities.

The book’s subtitle, “A Social Genealogy of the Human Soul,” should be taken seriously. Pettit is not merely using “soul” ornamentally. He is asking what it is about human beings that makes us judging, reasoning, valuing, responsible, self-identifying persons. But the genealogy is counterfactual and philosophical rather than an empirical origin story. The OUP description says that Pettit’s hypothesis is that our distinguishing capacities would emerge with speech and converse, and that such a genealogy can explicate their nature and function even if it does not describe actual historical origins.

The book identifies six major capacities. Three concern informational processing: intentional belief or attitude-updating, intentional reasoning, and intentional perception in which appearance and reality can be distinguished. Three concern relational or normative life: standards, values, and commitments; accountability and responsibility; and the status of persons with self-identity. Pettit’s thesis is that these capacities emerge as social skills and are then internalized in thought.

This gives us the most complete Pettitian model of human mindedness:

A human being is not merely an organism with representations. A human being is a conversive agent: one who can make judgments, offer reasons, ask and answer challenges, distinguish how things seem from how they are, bind herself through commitments, recognize standards, hold and be held responsible, and identify herself as a person over time.

The soul, in Pettit’s mature sense, is this conversive form of life.

8. Judgment: belief becomes answerable

Judgment is central because it changes the status of belief. A merely routinized creature may represent the world and act on those representations. A judging subject can take a stand on how things are. It can avow, defend, revise, and regulate belief in light of evidence and challenge.

“The Self-Regulating Mind” already gives a powerful account of this. McGeer and Pettit argue that judgment helps explain self-knowledge: when someone asks herself whether she believes that p, she does not inspect an inner object but considers whether p is true or whether the evidence supports p. Judgment can put belief in place. This is a familiar transparency thought—questions about belief often become questions about the world—but Pettit embeds it in a self-regulation theory. The judging subject does not merely discover belief; she can regulate herself into belief under norms of evidence.

This is also where normativity enters the mind internally. In self-regulating minds, belief is not only a causal-dispositional state. It is a normative commitment. McGeer and Pettit argue that expressible belief involves taking a position and being ready to defend it; such minds can be held responsible for their beliefs.

This is a major step toward the “soul” view. The human soul is not a hidden chamber behind behavior. It is the form of mindedness in which one’s attitudes become answerable to reasons, to oneself, and to others.

9. Reasoning: from causal transition to rule-governed movement

Pettit distinguishes mere psychological transition from reasoning. A creature may move from one state to another because of associative mechanisms, conditioning, or learned dispositions. Reasoning, by contrast, requires that contents are attended to as premises and conclusion under some rule or standard.

This is especially clear in “Consciousness Incorporated.” Pettit argues that reasoning and deliberation require coawareness: the subject must attend to intentional contents in a way that allows them to function as reasons. Reasoning is not just the occurrence of an input-output transition. It involves attending to contents and moving under a rule to a conclusion.

This distinction is crucial for any Pettitian philosophy of mind. Functional role alone can identify beliefs and desires at one level, but rational consciousness requires a further layer: contents must be present as reasons. This does not make reasoning non-natural. It means that a natural account of reasoning must include the normative and attentional structure by which contents are treated as inferentially relevant.

The result is a layered view:

At the first level, there are representation-guided transitions.

At the second level, there are intentional attitudes with functional roles.

At the third level, there is conscious reasoning: contents are coaware, linguistically articulable, and norm-governed.

At the fourth level, there is social reasoning: the subject can offer, request, contest, and revise reasons in converse with others.

Pettit’s human mind is built from all four levels.

10. Perception: appearance, reality, and qualitative consciousness

Pettit’s treatment of perception is one of the most delicate parts of his philosophy of mind because it approaches the traditional problem of phenomenal consciousness. He does not deny that there is “something it is like” to perceive. But he resists making independent, ineffable qualia the foundation of consciousness.

In When Minds Converse, one of the six major capacities is “perception with percipience,” meaning perception in which the subject can distinguish how things appear from how things are. This is not merely sensory intake. It is perception transformed by the capacity to judge, question, and revise. The perceiver can say, in effect: “It looks this way, but perhaps it is not this way.”

The 2019 John Locke lecture handout is especially revealing. Pettit says that judgment gives a maker’s knowledge of belief: when one judges or reasons to a conclusion, the representation is “for me—not just in me.” Perception, by contrast, gives a taker’s knowledge and has an intuitive qualitative character. He acknowledges that even unreasoning perceivers may have something the perceived world is like for them.

But he then asks whether a quale must be an independent ingredient of perception or whether the exercise of judgmental capacity gives perception its qualitative character. Pettit says he is inclined toward the latter, more parsimonious view: the quale may be derivative from the way we perceptually read the world rather than an independent ontological item. He also admits that this does not settle all cases, especially bodily sensation.

This is one of the few places where Pettit comes close to a direct theory of phenomenal consciousness. The cautious reconstruction is this: Pettit does not build his philosophy of mind around qualia; he does not claim to have solved every problem of bodily sensation or raw feel; but he tends to treat qualitative consciousness, at least in perception, as bound up with contentful, world-directed, judgment-sensitive awareness rather than with intrinsic private mental paint.

That makes him closer to an intentionalist or access-oriented theorist of consciousness than to a qualia-first phenomenal realist.

11. Consciousness as coawareness

“Consciousness Incorporated” is Pettit’s most explicit discussion of consciousness. It asks whether incorporated groups can have collective consciousness. To answer that, Pettit first distinguishes two conceptions of consciousness. One is awareness of content and reasons; the other is intrinsic phenomenal feel or quale, independent of accessible content. Pettit focuses on the former while remaining uncommitted on the latter.

He proposes the term “coawareness” for the kind of consciousness that matters to reasoning and deliberation. The “co” signals that consciousness is not a free-floating glow but an accompaniment of an intentional state or act with content. The content has resonance for the subject because it gives reasons. Pettit explicitly says he is focusing on coawareness and not committing himself on content-independent phenomenal consciousness or on whether incorporation could trigger that kind of consciousness.

He distinguishes three forms of coawareness: propositional, perceptual, and apperceptive.

Propositional coawareness occurs when a subject is aware of a proposition as something believed, considered, inferred, or used as a reason. This is central to judgment and reasoning.

Perceptual coawareness occurs when the subject is aware of worldly items or appearances in perception. Pettit allows that animals may enjoy perceptual coawareness, though the human form is enriched by language and judgment.

Apperceptive coawareness is self-awareness: not merely awareness of an object or proposition, but awareness of oneself as the subject whose attitude or act it is. In the group case, Pettit argues that incorporation can introduce a new apperceptive coawareness, a “who we are” standpoint for a collective subject.

A central claim follows: functional belief does not automatically entail consciousness. Pettit explicitly separates the functional conditions for propositional attitudes from coawareness, and he uses simple robot cases to show that a system might exhibit belief-like functional organization without conscious coawareness.

The philosophical importance is large. Pettit avoids two mistakes. He does not reduce mind to consciousness, because there can be intentional states without coawareness. But he also does not reduce consciousness to mere information processing, because coawareness requires that content be present to the subject as reason-giving. Consciousness is not just representation; it is reason-resonant representation.

12. Does Pettit have a theory of phenomenal consciousness?

Yes, but only a partial and restrained one. He is not primarily a hard-problem theorist. His positive theory is about coawareness: contentful, reason-giving, accessible consciousness. He explicitly brackets independent phenomenal consciousness in “Consciousness Incorporated.”

Still, he does not dismiss the “what it is like” dimension. In the same paper he says that even if phenomenal consciousness is real and daunting, access-consciousness or coawareness also ensures that there is something it is like to be in such states. He later argues that when attitudes are held with coawareness, there is certainly something it is like for the subject to hold them, in contrast with unconscious attitudes discovered only by third parties.

The best interpretation is that Pettit treats phenomenal character as at least partly dependent on the subject’s access to content as meaningful, reason-giving, and self-involving. He does not deny that raw feels might exist. He refuses to make them theoretically basic for his account of human mindedness. His 2019 handout suggests a stronger leaning: at least in perception, qualitative character may be derivative from our judgmental and perceptual reading of the world.

This creates a disciplined but incomplete philosophy of consciousness. It is disciplined because it gives a clear account of the form of consciousness required for judgment, reasoning, responsibility, and personhood. It is incomplete because it does not fully explain bodily feeling, pain, mood, affective tone, dream experience, or nonconceptual animal consciousness. Pettit would likely treat these as real phenomena, but they are not the organizing center of his philosophy.

13. Self-regulation, self-knowledge, and the responsible mind

Pettit’s human mind is self-regulating. This means more than self-monitoring. A self-regulating mind can represent a norm, constraint, or standard as such and can adjust itself in light of it. Human beings do not merely have beliefs; they can ask whether they ought to believe. They do not merely desire; they can ask whether a desire is defensible. They do not merely act; they can take responsibility for acting.

McGeer and Pettit’s “Self-Regulating Mind” treats belief as normatively structured. They argue that the distinction between routinized and self-regulating minds helps explain judgment, self-knowledge, belief as normative commitment, self-deception, hope, trust, and the developmental role of folk psychology. Folk psychology, on this view, is not merely a map for predicting behavior. It also trains young minds into self-regulation.

That last point is profound. The vocabulary of belief, desire, reason, intention, excuse, responsibility, promise, and commitment does not merely describe a pre-existing mind. It helps make a certain kind of mind possible. Children become persons partly by being addressed as persons, asked for reasons, corrected, trusted, blamed, forgiven, and taught to avow and revise commitments.

This is where Pettit’s philosophy of mind becomes social developmental anthropology. The mind is not first fully formed and then socially interpreted. It becomes the kind of mind it is through social interpretation and discipline.

14. Free will as discursive and conversive control

Pettit’s account of free will is continuous with this philosophy of mind. In A Theory of Freedom, he begins from the psychological description of a person as a free subject and connects free agency, free will, and political freedom. Freedom in the subject is tied to being fit to be held responsible and authorized as a partner in interaction. A Notre Dame review summarizes the view as freedom constituted by “discursive control,” involving rational and relational capacities in non-dominative discourse.

This is not libertarian metaphysics in the traditional sense. Pettit does not ground free will in an uncaused inner power. Nor is it simple compatibilist voluntariness. Freedom is a status and capacity within practices of reason-giving. One is free when one can be addressed, challenged, exhorted, and held responsible under conditions that do not subject one to domination.

When Minds Converse refines this further. Pettit connects responsibility and free will with shared evaluative norms and “expectational discipline.” The relevant form of exhortation is “you should and can”; if the agent fails without excuses of rationality, knowledge, or voluntariness, blame becomes appropriate. Free will, in the intuitive sense, is constituted by the willpower needed to comply with such exhortation.

Thus, free will belongs to the human soul not as metaphysical exemption from causality but as conversive answerability. A free agent is someone who can be reached by reasons, can regulate herself under norms, and can be held responsible by others who treat her as a partner in discourse.

15. Personhood and self-identity

Pettit’s person is not merely a biological individual. Personhood is a normative and social status achieved in practices of converse, commitment, and accountability. This follows from his Hobbesian work, where persons are roles constituted through language and representation, and from When Minds Converse, where personhood and self-identity are among the six capacities generated by conversive practice.

The self, in this framework, is not a Cartesian ego directly present to itself before all social relation. It is also not a fiction. It is a practical and apperceptive structure: the subject who can own attitudes, make commitments, answer for actions, recognize herself over time, and be recognized by others as the same accountable person.

Pettit’s notion of apperceptive coawareness helps here. In individual consciousness, apperception is the awareness of oneself as the subject of thought and action. In collective agency, it becomes a “we” standpoint: the group can be aware of itself as the subject whose judgments, commitments, and actions are at stake. Pettit argues that incorporated bodies can develop such a collective self-standpoint, a robust “who we are” that plays a role analogous to individual self-awareness.

This gives us a non-mysterious but robust account of selfhood. A self is not an inner substance but a normatively organized standpoint of ownership and answerability.

16. Collective minds and group consciousness

Pettit’s theory of group agency is not incidental to his philosophy of mind. With Christian List, he argues that group or corporate agents can really exist over and above individual agents, though in an unmysterious way grounded in social organization, decision procedures, and commitment structures. Their position lies between a Hobbesian view that reduces group agency to a spokesperson and a romantic view that imagines a higher spiritual force.

“Consciousness Incorporated” asks the natural next question: if groups can be intentional agents, can they be conscious? Pettit’s answer depends on the type of consciousness. He is skeptical or noncommittal about whether incorporation could generate content-independent phenomenal consciousness. But he argues that corporate bodies can enjoy consciousness in the sense of propositional coawareness, because they can reason about what to think, seek, or do.

He also argues that collective apperceptive coawareness can emerge: incorporated groups can develop a “we” standpoint. Perceptual coawareness is more limited. Large corporations do not literally perceive as unified organisms in the same way persons do, though small-scale joint action may require joint perceptual awareness, as in coordinated action where participants act together in a shared perceptual field.

The resulting view is subtle:

Groups can have beliefs, desires, intentions, reasons, commitments, and forms of self-awareness.

Groups can be conscious in the access/coawareness sense.

Groups probably do not have independent phenomenal consciousness or a unified experiential feel like an organism.

Groups are not mystical collective souls, but some are genuine subjects of judgment and action.

This is one of Pettit’s most distinctive contributions to contemporary philosophy of mind. He extends intentionality and consciousness beyond the individual organism without spiritualizing the collective.

17. Respect, conversability, and the “soul”

The theological dimension becomes clearest in Pettit’s theory of respect. In “A Conversive Theory of Respect,” Pettit argues that our command of language gives us a special means of mutual influence: we can address one another through shared standards of reasons rather than merely causally manipulating one another. Respectful action treats another as a conversive partner.

The ground of respect, on this view, is conversability: the capacity to engage with one another in reason-governed, commitment-bearing converse. Pettit explicitly contrasts this with theories that ground respect in an intrinsic non-social property such as autonomy or “having a soul.”

This line is theologically important and potentially controversial. Pettit is not merely ignoring theological accounts of the soul. He is offering an alternative to them. Where some traditions ground the dignity of persons in an intrinsic soul, Pettit grounds respect in a social-relational capacity: the ability to stand with others in a space of reasons, claims, commitments, and accountability.

But this should not be read as a crude secular dismissal of the soul. Pettit’s later book uses the phrase “human soul” because he is trying to explain the same domain that soul-talk historically marked: the special character of human personhood. The difference is that he explains it genealogically and socially rather than metaphysically and theologically.

A theological reader could respond in at least three ways.

One could reject Pettit’s account as insufficient because it replaces an intrinsic metaphysical ground of dignity with a socially dependent capacity.

One could appropriate it as a naturalistic account of how ensouled life is formed in history, language, and community, while preserving theological claims about the soul at another level.

One could use Pettit to revise theology itself, treating the soul less as an isolated substance and more as a relational, communicative, accountable form of life.

The third option is closest to Pettit’s own philosophical spirit, though not necessarily to his explicit commitments. He gives us a secular, social, language-centered anthropology that can converse with theology without becoming theology.

18. The non-English reception: what it adds

The non-English reception does not overturn the Anglophone reading, but it sharpens it in useful ways.

French discussions emphasize Pettit’s weak holism and the refusal of both atomism and collectivism. This is valuable because it guards against a common misreading of the later “society-first” thesis. Pettit is not saying that society thinks instead of individuals. He is saying that individuals become the thinking beings they are through social conditions.

Portuguese reception has explicitly linked Pettit’s holist-individualist epistemology with his republican political theory. That connection is central: the same thinker who says mind is socially formed also says freedom requires protection from domination. Social constitution is not subjection.

Spanish-language discussion of his philosophy of mind has highlighted his functionalist account of intentional states and his interpretation of Hobbesian language as a human creation rather than an intrinsic natural endowment. This supports the reading that Pettit’s human soul is neither a biological given nor a supernatural insertion. It is a language-shaped achievement.

The main value of this multilingual reception is negative as well as positive: it helps prevent three distortions. Pettit is not a Cartesian dualist, not a reductionist eliminativist, and not a collectivist mystic. He is a social naturalist of mind.

19. Extracting a comprehensive Pettitian philosophy of mind

A systematic Pettitian philosophy of mind can now be stated as follows.

The ontology of mind is naturalistic and functional. Minds are systems of representation-guided, goal-directed activity. They do not require mental substance or non-natural causal powers. Human minds are special not because they contain a different metaphysical ingredient but because they acquire self-regulating, language-mediated, norm-governed capacities.

The individuation of mental content is broad and social. Intentional states are not always individuated by what is inside the head. Their content may depend on worldly relations, linguistic practices, and social embedding. Human thought is therefore not private first and social later; it is social in its very conditions of possibility.

The reality of mental states is secured by functional and program-explanatory roles. Beliefs and desires are real because they occupy stable roles in explanation, prediction, self-regulation, and social interaction. They need not be microphysical causes to be causally and explanatorily relevant. They program patterns of lower-level realization.

The human mind is self-regulating. Human beings can represent standards as standards, regulate belief through judgment, treat belief as commitment, and hold themselves answerable. Folk psychology participates in forming such minds, especially developmentally.

Language is transformative. Speech does not merely express pre-existing rationality; it creates the social space in which judgment, reasoning, personhood, commitment, and accountability can emerge. Human beings are not just minded animals that speak; they are speaking animals whose minds become distinctively human through converse.

Consciousness is primarily coawareness. Conscious states are those in which contents are present to a subject as reason-giving and action-guiding. Coawareness comes in propositional, perceptual, and apperceptive forms. Pettit brackets independent phenomenal consciousness, but he does not deny that there is something it is like to be in conscious states.

Perception is judgment-sensitive. Human perception is not merely sensory registration; it involves the capacity to distinguish appearance from reality and to integrate perceptual content into reason-governed judgment. Qualitative character may be derivative from this perceptual reading of the world, though Pettit leaves some problems unresolved.

The self is a standpoint of ownership and accountability. It is neither an immaterial ego nor a fiction. It is the apperceptive and normative structure through which a subject can own beliefs, intentions, actions, and commitments over time.

Free will is discursive control. A free agent is one who can be addressed by reasons, exhorted, held responsible, and participate in non-dominating discourse. Free will is not an uncaused power but the capacity for responsible self-regulation under shared norms.

Personhood is conversive. Persons are beings who can enter into reason-governed relations of commitment, recognition, and accountability. Respect is grounded not in an isolated intrinsic metaphysical property but in conversability.

The soul is the socially generated form of human mindedness. Pettit’s “human soul” is not a detachable immaterial entity. It is the ensemble of language-mediated capacities by which natural minds become judging, reasoning, valuing, responsible, self-identifying persons.

Collective minds are possible. Organized groups can be intentional agents with beliefs, desires, intentions, commitments, and forms of coawareness. They may have propositional and apperceptive consciousness, though probably not organism-like phenomenal consciousness.

Politics follows from mind. Pettit’s republicanism is not an external application of his philosophy of mind. If persons are conversive agents, then domination is a violation of their status as reason-giving, reason-responsive subjects. The move from mind to respect, and from respect to non-domination, is internal to the theory. In When Minds Converse, Pettit explicitly connects the genealogy’s ideal conditions with equality, respect, and freedom as non-domination.

20. Theological interpretation: what Pettit’s “soul” can and cannot mean

Pettit’s use of “soul” should be read as philosophically serious but theologically revisionary.

It is serious because he is not merely talking about cognition in a thin computational sense. He is talking about the capacities that make human beings persons: judgment, reason, perception as appearance-sensitive, values, responsibility, free will, selfhood, and respect. These are precisely the phenomena that theological anthropology often gathers under the heading of soul.

It is revisionary because Pettit explains these capacities through social genealogy rather than divine creation, immaterial substance, or innate rational essence. The human soul is made possible by speech, converse, commitment, and recognition. This is not the soul as separable substance; it is the soul as socially formed personhood.

The relation to theology is therefore complex.

Pettit is opposed to grounding respect simply in “having a soul” if that means appealing to a private, intrinsic, non-social property. His conversive theory of respect explicitly contrasts itself with that kind of account.

But his account may be attractive to theological traditions that emphasize relational personhood, covenant, confession, testimony, promise, command, answerability, and communal formation. Pettit’s soul is not silent inwardness; it is answerable life. It is the being who can say “I,” hear “you,” form “we,” accept “ought,” and stand under reasons.

Where classical theology may say that the soul is what makes a human being capable of communion with God and neighbor, Pettit gives a secular analogue: the soul is what makes a human being capable of converse, commitment, responsibility, and respect. The analogy is not identity. Pettit does not give us immortality, grace, sin, salvation, divine image, or resurrection. But he does give a powerful account of why speech, recognition, and accountability are not superficial social add-ons but constitutive of human personhood.

A theological critique would press him on cases where conversability is absent, impaired, latent, or not yet developed: infants, people with severe cognitive disabilities, the comatose, the dying, and nonhuman animals. Pettit’s conversive grounding of respect is elegant but risks making dignity depend too closely on a capacity that not all human beings can exercise. His broader account can answer partly by appealing to social identity, potentiality, recognition, and membership in a human form of life, but this is a pressure point. Traditional soul-theories often have an advantage here because they can ground dignity independently of manifest conversational capacity.

21. Strengths of Pettit’s philosophy of mind

The first strength is integration. Pettit connects philosophy of mind, language, action theory, social ontology, ethics, and politics. Mind is not sealed off from personhood, and personhood is not sealed off from political freedom.

The second strength is his anti-reductionist naturalism. Program explanation allows him to respect lower-level causal science while preserving the explanatory autonomy of mental and social categories. This is one of the most powerful parts of the system.

The third strength is his developmental and social realism. Human beings really are formed through speech, correction, accountability, trust, and shared norms. Pettit’s theory explains why a child’s entry into language is also entry into a new form of mind.

The fourth strength is his account of consciousness as reason-resonant coawareness. It gives a clear account of the kind of consciousness required for judgment, reasoning, and responsibility, without getting trapped in purely private qualia.

The fifth strength is his theory of collective mind. Pettit can explain how corporations, committees, states, and institutions become genuine agents without invoking mystical group spirits. This is increasingly important in a world where collective agents make decisions with enormous moral and political consequences.

22. Limits and unresolved problems

The first limit is phenomenal consciousness. Pettit’s account of coawareness is powerful, but it does not fully address the hard problem of consciousness. Bodily sensation, affect, pain, mood, and nonconceptual animal experience remain underdeveloped. His 2019 handout acknowledges that bodily sensation and related cases are not fully resolved by treating qualia as derivative from perceptual reading.

The second limit is the risk of over-linguistic intellectualism. Pettit’s society-first view is plausible for judgment, responsibility, and personhood, but less obviously adequate for prelinguistic emotion, embodied skill, trauma, imagination, music, ritual, and forms of understanding that are not primarily discursive.

The third limit is dignity beyond conversability. If respect is grounded in conversive capacity, then hard questions arise about human beings who cannot converse. Pettit’s theory needs supplementation to avoid excluding those whose personhood is not expressed through standard adult linguistic agency.

The fourth limit is empirical genealogy. Pettit is clear that When Minds Converse gives a counterfactual philosophical genealogy rather than a precise origin story. That is legitimate, but it means the theory must be connected cautiously to anthropology, developmental psychology, comparative cognition, and evolutionary linguistics.

The fifth limit is group consciousness. Pettit’s access-consciousness account makes group consciousness plausible, but critics who identify consciousness primarily with phenomenal subjectivity will remain unconvinced. Pettit’s groups may be conscious in the coawareness sense without there being anything it is like to be the corporation.

The sixth limit is theology. Pettit’s “soul” is philosophically rich but secular. A theologian can learn from it, but cannot simply identify it with traditional doctrines of the soul without further argument.

23. Final synthesis

Philip Pettit’s philosophy of mind can be distilled into one governing idea: the distinctively human mind is a socially formed, linguistically mediated, norm-governed, self-regulating mode of natural intentional agency.

At the base are intentional systems: organisms or agents that represent and act. At the human level, language transforms this base into judgment, reasoning, answerability, self-knowledge, responsibility, and personhood. Consciousness is not primarily an inner glow but coawareness: contents present to a subject as reasons. The self is not an immaterial ego but an apperceptive standpoint of ownership and accountability. Free will is not contra-causal magic but discursive control. Respect is not grounded in a hidden metaphysical substance but in the capacity for conversive partnership. The soul, finally, is the human form of life that emerges when natural minds become speaking, judging, responsible persons.

Pettit’s achievement is to make “soul” compatible with naturalism without making it shallow. His account is not a theology of the soul, but it is a serious secular anthropology of ensouled life: life lived in the first and second person, under reasons, in language, before others, with responsibility.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
